title: Anthropic expands Project Glasswing to 150 critical-infrastructure organizations in 15+ countries
subtitle: Claude Mythos found 10,000 high-severity flaws with the initial 50 partners — now power, water, healthcare, and communications operators are getting access.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Anthropic announced on June 2, 2026, that it is expanding Project Glasswing — its restricted cybersecurity program built around the unreleased Claude Mythos model — to roughly 150 additional organizations across more than 15 countries. The original cohort of about 50 partners, including AWS, Apple, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorgan Chase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, and Nvidia, deployed Claude Mythos Preview in April to scan their codebases. In one month they surfaced more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities. Cloudflare alone found 2,000 bugs; Mozilla fixed 271 in a single Firefox release, more than ten times what Claude Opus 4.6 had caught a version earlier.

The expansion broadens the program into sectors that were underrepresented in the original cohort — power, water, healthcare, communications, and hardware — and adds critical open-source maintainers and third-party safety testers. Anthropic is also releasing the internal tooling that Glasswing partners use to triage Mythos findings, and is scaling up a Cyber Verification Program that will eventually grant Mythos-class capabilities to organizations for specific defensive tasks. The structural argument from Anthropic is that frontier coding models have crossed the threshold where they can outperform all but the most skilled human auditors at finding exploitable bugs — and the only stable equilibrium is to put that capability in defenders' hands faster than it diffuses into attackers'.

This sits at the intersection of two stories the feed has tracked all spring: the EU AI Act simplification (which extended high-risk system deadlines but added new prohibitions), and the new US executive order from June 2 that creates an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse. Glasswing is functionally the private-sector version of what that clearinghouse would coordinate. The interesting tension is patch velocity — Anthropic has openly said that open-source maintainers are becoming the bottleneck, because Mythos can generate detailed vulnerability reports faster than human teams can review and fix them.

For learners: this is what an offense-defense imbalance looks like in real time. The career path that's opening up isn't running the scanner — Mythos does that — it's the judgment work around it: triaging which of 10,000 findings is exploitable in your specific environment, sequencing patches against business risk, and writing the disclosure timeline that maintainers can actually meet. The scanner is the cheap part. The reasoning around it isn't.
